skill.name: writing-shape
skill.description: Take a markdown file of raw material and shape it into an article through a conversational session — drafting candidate openings, growing the piece paragraph by paragraph, arguing about format (lists, tables, callouts, quotes) at each step. Use when the user has a pile of notes, fragments, or a rough draft and wants help turning it into something publishable.
skill.summary: Conversational article-shaping session that turns raw markdown notes into a publishable article, paragraph by paragraph.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: The skill is a benign writing assistant that shapes raw markdown into articles through conversation. All six static findings are false positives: the 'weak cryptographic algorithm' alerts are misfires on prose words like 'paragraph'; the 'Ruby/shell backtick' alerts match markdown inline-code formatting (e.g., `> [!TIP]`), not executable code; and the 'system reconnaissance' alert matches a benign file-path question to the user. No code is executed, no network or filesystem risk exists beyond reading and writing user-specified markdown files.

content.user_title: Shape raw notes into a publishable article
content.value_statement: Writers often sit on piles of fragmented notes without knowing how to begin an article. This skill runs a conversational shaping session that drafts candidate openings, grows the piece paragraph by paragraph, and argues about format choices until the article is done.
actual_capabilities: Reads a user-supplied markdown file of raw material end-to-end before drafting
Drafts 2–3 candidate openings with different thesis angles and forces a choice
Grows the article one paragraph or block at a time, preserving the user's in-session edits
Argues explicitly about format tradeoffs (prose vs. list, inline vs. callout, table vs. repeated structure, quote vs. paraphrase, code block vs. inline)
Re-reads the article file from disk before each write to avoid overwriting user edits
Names gaps explicitly when the raw material lacks something the article needs
limitations: Does not mine for new fragments outside the supplied pile — gaps must be filled by the user
Does not edit the raw material file, which is treated as read-only input
Does not add publishing frontmatter or platform-specific formatting unless the user asks
Output quality depends on the quality and completeness of the input pile

use_cases.title: Turn scattered notes into a blog post
use_cases.description: A developer has a markdown dump of half-formed ideas and wants a coherent blog post drafted step by step with editorial pushback.

use_cases.title: Reshape a rough draft into a clear article
use_cases.description: A technical writer has an unstructured draft and wants help restructuring it paragraph by paragraph while keeping their own voice.

use_cases.title: Co-author an article with explicit format arguments
use_cases.description: A content lead wants an AI collaborator that challenges format choices (lists, tables, callouts) rather than silently picking one.
use_cases.target_user: Content lead or editor working with an AI co-author

prompt_templates.title: Basic shaping session
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a markdown file at ./notes.md with rough material. Start a shaping session: read it, then draft 2–3 candidate openings so I can pick one.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner kicking off a shaping session from a notes file

prompt_templates.title: Open a pile from a specific path
prompt_templates.prompt: Read ./research/interview-transcript.md as the raw pile and save the shaped article to ./articles/interview.md. Walk me through it.
prompt_templates.scenario: User supplies explicit input and output paths

prompt_templates.title: Format-argumentation mode
prompt_templates.prompt: For the next beat, argue out loud whether it should be a paragraph, a list, or a table. I want the tradeoff, not a silent choice.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants the skill to surface format decisions explicitly

prompt_templates.title: Gap-naming and cutting
prompt_templates.prompt: If the pile is missing an example or a claim, name the gap immediately and offer to cut the section. Don't invent material I didn't provide.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user enforcing honest sourcing from the pile

output_examples.input: I passed a markdown dump of fragmented thoughts about debugging strategies. The skill read it, then offered three openings.
output: Opening A: 'Debugging is mostly reading' — argues the bottleneck is comprehension, not code.
Opening B: 'Three habits that made me faster at debugging' — list-style, practical.
Opening C: 'Why I stopped using print statements' — contrarian, specific.

output_examples.input: I asked what format the next beat should be, given a paragraph about API error types.
output: Prose vs. list: 'These error types are genuinely parallel (4xx vs. 5xx), so a table carries the comparison faster than prose.'
Table proposal: 3 columns — Code, Meaning, Retry? — with one row per error class.

best_practices: Read the pile in full before drafting any opening so openings are grounded in the material, not invented.
Append each agreed paragraph or block to the article file immediately so the user can see progress and edit between turns.
Re-read the article file from disk before every write to preserve the user's manual edits during the session.
anti_patterns: Do not edit the raw material file — it is read-only input to this skill.
Do not invent fragments that are not in the pile; name the gap and let the user fill it or cut the section.
Do not silently pick a format (list, table, callout) — argue the tradeoff out loud with the user.

faq.question: Does this skill edit my raw notes file?
faq.answer: No. The raw material file is treated as read-only input. The skill writes a separate article file.

faq.question: Where does the article get saved?
faq.answer: If the user does not specify a path, the skill asks once and remembers it for the rest of the session.

faq.question: Will it overwrite my manual edits to the article?
faq.answer: No. The skill re-reads the article file from disk before every write, so edits you make between turns are preserved.

faq.question: Can it add material that is not in my notes?
faq.answer: No. If the article needs something the pile does not contain, the skill names the gap and asks you to supply it or cut the section.

faq.question: Does it handle publishing or platform formatting?
faq.answer: No. Publishing, frontmatter, and platform-specific formatting are out of scope unless you explicitly ask.

faq.question: What tools is this skill compatible with?
faq.answer: It works with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as listed in the supported tools.
